content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 27, 2017

Kenneth R. Hetge
Tehachapi City Councilman Elect
333 S. Dennison Road, #65
Tehachapi, CA 93561

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-16-264

Dear Mr. Hetge:

This letter responds to Tehachapi City Councilmember Kenneth R. Hetge's request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act") and Section 1090.¹ Please note that we do not advise on any other area of law, including Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of interest. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.

In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Kern County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTIONS

1. Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Hetge from taking part in governmental decisions related to the development and construction of a motocross track on the City's Event Center & Rodeo Grounds within the boundaries of the Tehachapi Municipal Airport (hereafter, the "Motocross Track Project")?

2. Does Section 1090 prohibit the Councilmember from making or participating in the making of, or the City from entering into, a potential contract or contracts relating to the development, construction, or operation of the Motocross Track Project?

___________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-264
Page No. 2

CONCLUSIONS

1. No. The Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in decisions related to
the development and construction of the Project because those decisions would not have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the Councilmember's financial interests.

2. No. Section 1090 does not prohibit the Councilmember from making or participating in
the making of, or the City from entering into, a potential contract or contracts relating to the
development, construction, or operation of the Project.

FACTS

Tehachapi City Councilmember Kenneth Hetge was elected to office on November 8, 2016,
and his four-year term of office commenced on December 19, 2016.

The City owns the Tehachapi Municipal Airport ("TMA"), the TMA is located within the
City limits, and the TMA is currently home to 95 aircraft and 80 hangars. The Councilmember and
his wife, as partners, have owned and operated an aviation business located on TMA land for over
10 years. The Councilmember and his wife are the only employees of the business, they have
invested substantially more than $2,000 in the business, and each assists in the management of the
business.

The Councilmember's business leases hangars, rents aircraft, and provides aircraft-
maintenance services. The business owns six hangars located on TMA land, and it leases the TMA
land underlying those hangars pursuant to long term leases with the City. These long-term leases
are for 20-year terms with two 5-year extensions available to the lessee, and the oldest of these
leases were entered into between 2003 and 2004.

The Councilmember's business subleases four of its six hangars to tenants, and its aircraft-
rental and aircraft-maintenance operations are conducted out of the remaining two hangars.
According to a telephone conversation with the Councilmember on January 19, 2017, none of the
business's tenants, nor its aircraft-rental customers, nor its aircraft-maintenance customers conducts
any business relating to the Motocross Track Project to the best of his knowledge.

The City Council will soon consider decisions relating to the development and construction
of the Motocross Track Project on the City's Event Center & Rodeo Grounds within the boundaries
of the TMA. The City owns the lands underlying the TMA, including those underlying the Event
Center & Rodeo Grounds and the potential motocross track. According to correspondence from the
City Manager, Mr. Greg Garrett, dated January 10, 2017, the decisions relating to the Project
include the following:

• Should the City remove the property for the motocross track and event center from
within the boundaries of the TMA?

File No. A-16-264
Page No. 3

• Should the City keep the property within the TMA boundaries but pursue a decision from the Federal Aviation Administration ("FAA") to designate it as permanent "non-aviation" or "revenue supporting" property while still abiding by the FAA's rules regarding property development?

• Should the City keep the property within the TMA boundaries but remove all Federal obligations from the property thereby eliminating the need to follow the FAA's rules regarding property development?

• Should the City abandon the development of the motocross track and future improvement of the existing Event Center?

In that correspondence, with respect to potential contracts relating to the Project, the City Manager stated as follows: "At this time, there are no contracts being presented to the Council regarding this issue. That said, the possible long-term development of the motocross track and event center in question will likely lead to contracts for the operation of the facility by a third party entity."

ANALYSIS

The Act

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in the making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have material financial effect on one or more of the public official's interests identified in that section. Of the interests identified in Section 87103, the Councilmember's interests implicated by the decisions relating to the Motocross Track Project are the following:

• Any business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment worth $2,000 or more or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(a) and (d).) The Councilmember has an interest in his aviation business because he and his wife own the business, they have invested in excess of $2,000 in the business, and each assists in the management of the business.

• Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).) The Councilmember has an interest in his business's leaseholds of TMA land. The business owns six hangars located on TMA land, and it leases the TMA land underlying the hangars pursuant to long-term leases with the City.

• Any source of income, including promised income, totaling $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) The Councilmember has an interest in sources of income to his business, including the business's hangar tenants, aircraft-rental customers, and aircraft-maintenance customers.

File No. A-16-264
Page No. 4

• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate family. (Section 87103.) An official always has an interest in his or her personal finances.²

Foreseeability and Materiality

The Councilmember's interests are not explicitly involved in decisions regarding the Motocross Track Project.³ Therefore, if the financial effect of the decisions at issue can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. (Regulation 1870l(b).)

The Councilmember's Aviation Business

Regulation 18702.1(b) provides that a decision's effect on a business interest is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the effect would contribute to a change in the value of the privately-held business at issue. At issue is whether the decisions relating to the Motocross Track Project would contribute to a change in the value of the Councilmember's aviation business.

The decisions relating to the Project may result in its development and construction on the Event Center grounds within the boundaries of the TMA. The Councilmember's business, located on TMA lands, leases hangars, rents aircraft, and provides aircraft-maintenance services. The Project may result in additional visitors, traffic, and commercial activity related to the motocross track. However, there is no indication that the additional visitors, traffic, and commercial activity would result in any change in demand for the aviation services provided by the Councilmember's business.

Therefore, we find that the effect of the decisions relating to the Project would not contribute to a change in the value of the Councilmember's business.

The Business's Leaseholds

Regulation 18702.2(b) provides that a decision's effect on any real property in which the official has a leasehold interest is material if the decision will: (1) change the termination date of the lease; (2) increase or decrease the potential rental value of the property; (3) increase or decrease the rental value of the property, and the official has a right to sublease the property; (4) change the official's actual or legally allowable use of the real property; or (5) impact the official's use and enjoyment of the real property. At issue is whether the decisions relating to the Project would have a material effect on the Councilmember's business's leaseholds of TMA land.

² Regulation 18702.5(c) provides that if a decision affects a business entity or real property in which the official has an interest, materiality is determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1, relating to business entity interests, or Regulation 18702.2, relating to real property interests. Because the decisions at issue affect the Councilmember's business and real-property interests, we apply the materiality standards applicable to those interests, and we do not further address the Councilmember's interest in his personal finances or those of immediate family members.

³ An interest is explicitly involved if the interest is a named party in or the subject of the governmental decision. If an interest is explicitly involved the effect of the decision is presumed foreseeable. (Regulation 1870l(a).)

File No. A-16-264
Page No. 5

The business owns six hangars located on TMA land, and it leases the TMA land underlying the hangars pursuant to long-term leases with the City. These long-term leases are for 20-year terms with two five-year extensions available to the lessee on each lease, and the oldest of these leases were entered into between 2003 and 2004. Therefore, the earliest that one of the leases could expire or come up for renewal is between 2023 and 2024.

The decisions relating to the Project will not change the termination date of the leases. There is no indication that the decisions will increase or decrease the potential rental value of the leased TMA lands. The decisions would not change the Councilmember's actual or legally allowable use of the leased TMA lands, and there is no indication that the decisions would impact the Councilmember's use and enjoyment of the leased TMA lands.

Due to its proximity to the TMA, the motocross track could lead to noise pollution and safety issues, but these impacts are unlikely to affect the Councilmember's current use and enjoyment of the leased TMA lands because the TMA is already subject to substantial noise pollution due to the operation of aircraft, and any safety issues resultant from the potential Project are unlikely to deter the ongoing existing use of the TMA.

Therefore, we find that the decisions relating to the Project would not have a material financial effect on the Councilmember's business's leaseholds of TMA lands.

The Business's Sources of Income

With respect to income received by an official for services provided in the ordinary course of business, Regulation 18702.3(a) provides the applicable materiality standards. These standards vary depending on whether the source is directly or indirectly involved, and whether the source is an individual, business, or nonprofit.

The Councilmember's business subleases four of its six hangars to tenants, and its aircraft-rental and aircraft-maintenance operations are conducted out of the remaining two hangars. According to the Councilmember, none of the business's tenants, nor its aircraft-rental customers, nor its aircraft-maintenance customers conducts any business relating to the potential Project to the best of his knowledge.

In regard to the Motocross Track Project, sources of income to the Councilmember's business are not directly involved as the sources are not claimants, applicants, respondents, contracting parties, or otherwise named parties. Moreover, it does not appear that the decisions will have a foreseeable material effect on the sources provided you have no reason to know that the sources have other businesses or property interests implicated by the decisions at issue.

Thus, we conclude that the Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in decisions relating to the Project because those decisions would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of his interests.

File No. A-16-264
Page No. 6

Section 1090

Section 1090 "codifies the long-standing common law rule that barred public officials from
being personally financially interested in the contracts they formed in their official capacities."
(Lexin v. Superior Court (2010) 47 Cal.4th 1050, 1072.) "The prohibition is based on the rationale
that a person cannot effectively serve two masters at the same time." (Davis. v. Fresno Unified
School District (2015) 237 Cal.App.4th 261, 298.)

We employ the following six-step analysis to determine whether an official has a conflict of
interest under Section 1090.

Step One: Is the Councilmember subject to the provisions of Section 1090?

Section 1090 provides in pertinent part: "Members of the Legislature, state, county, district,
judicial district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract
made by them in their official capacity, or by anybody or board of which they are members."

Because the Councilmember is a city officer and a member of the City Council, he is subject
to Section 1090.

Step Two: Do decisions relating to the Motocross Track Project involve a contract or
contracts?

To determine whether a decision involves a contract, one should refer to general contract
principles. (See 89 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 258, 260 (2006); 84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36 (2001); 78
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 230, 234 (1995).)

The City Manager stated there are no contracts relating to the Project being presented to the
Council at this time. However, he also noted that if the City decides to continue to pursue the
development and construction of the Motocross Track Project, the end result of those efforts would
likely lead to contracts for the development, construction, and operation of the Project. Therefore,
these potential contracts relating to the Project are a foreseeable potential result of the decisions at
issue. Accordingly, we find that a contract or contracts are involved in the decisions at issue for
purposes of Section 1090.⁴

⁴ The Councilmember's business's long-term leases of the TMA lands underlying its hangars are not currently
at issue with respect to Section 1090 because the Councilmember was not in office when the leases were executed and
because none of those leases will come before the City for renewal before 2023, after the expiration of the
Councilmember's current term of office. However, we caution that the Councilmember should seek further advice
regarding the Section 1090's potential application to those leases if there is any indication that one or more of them may
come before the City Council for cancellation, modification, or renewal while the Councilmember holds that office.

File No. A-16-264
Page No. 7

Step Three: Is the Councilmember making or participating in making a contract or
contracts?

Where an official is a member of a board or commission that has the power to execute the
contract at issue, he or she is conclusively presumed to be involved in the making of his or her
agency's contracts irrespective of whether he or she actually participates in the making of the
contract. (Thomson, supra, at pp. 645 & 649; Fraser-Yamor Agency, Inc. v. County of Del Norte
(1977) 68 Cal.App.3d 201; 89 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 49 (2006).)

Accordingly, the Councilmember will be conclusively presumed to be involved in the
making of the potential contracts relating to the Project if the City Council decides to proceed with
its development, construction, and operation.

Step Four: Does the Councilmember have a financial interest in the potential contracts
relating to the Project?

Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has
a financial interest," and officials are deemed to have a financial interest in a contract if they might
profit from it in any way. (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) The phrase
"financially interested" broadly encompasses anything that would tie a public official's fortunes to
the existence of a public contract. (Carson Redevelopment Agency v. Padilla (2006) 140
Cal.App.4th 1323, 1334.) "The government's right to the absolute undivided allegiance of a public
officer is diminished as effectively where the officer acts with a hope of personal financial gain as
where he acts with certainty." (People v. Gnass (2002) 101 Cal.App.4th 1271, 1298 (citations
omitted).)

As noted above, the Councilmember's business, located on TMA lands, leases hangars,
rents aircraft, and provides aircraft-maintenance services. There is no indication that the
development, construction, or operation of the Project would result in any change in demand for the
aviation services provided by the Councilmember's business. Furthermore, the potential contracts
for the development, construction, and operation of the Project would not affect the term,
provisions, or value of the business's long-term leases of the TMA lands underlying the hangars.
Finally, it does not appear that the potential contracts relating to the Project would affect any source
of income to the Councilmember's business. Therefore, based on the facts presented, we find that
the Councilmember would not be financially interested in the potential contracts relating to the
Project for purposes of Section 1090.⁵

___________________________
⁵ However, if there is a change in the facts or circumstances relating to these potential contracts, we caution
that the Councilmember should seek further advice regarding the applicability of Section 1090 in light of those changed
facts or circumstances.

File No. A-16-264
Page No. 8

Thus, we conclude that Section 1090 does not prohibit the Councilmember from making or
participating in the making of, or the City from entering into, one or more potential contracts for the
development, construction, or operation of the Project. Because Section 1090 is not applicable, we
do not analyze whether a remote or noninterest exception or the rule of necessity applies, and our
Section-1090 analysis ends here.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl

U The Councilmember's business's long-tenn leases of the TMA lands underlying its hangars are not currently

because none of those leases will come before the City for renewal before 2023, after the expiration of the

regarding the Section 1090's potential application to those leases if there is any indication that one or more of them may

contracts?

contract at issue, he or she is conclusively presumed to be involved in the making of his or her 
agency's contracts irrespective of whether he or she actually participates in the making of the 
contract. (Thomson, supra, at pp. 645 & 649; Fraser-Yamor Agency, Inc. v. County of Del Norte 
(1977) 68 Cal.App.3d 201; 89 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 49 (2006).) 
Accordingly, the Councilmember will be conclusively presumed to be involved in the 
making of the potential contracts relating to the Project if the City Council decides to proceed with 
its development, construction, and operation. 
Step Four: Docs the Councilmcmbcr have a financial interest in the potential contracts 
relating to the Project? 
Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has 
a financial interest," and officials are deemed to have a financial interest in a contract if they might 
profit from it in any way. (People ,,. Honig ( 1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) The phrase 
"financially interested" broadly encompasses anything that would tie a public official's fortunes to 
the existence of a public contract. ( Carson Redevelopment Agency v. Padilla (2006) 140 
Cal.App.4th 1323, 1334.) "The government's right to the absolute undivided allegiance of a public 
officer is diminished as effectively where the officer acts with a hope of personal financial gain as 
where he acts with certainty." (People v. Gnass (2002) 101 Cal.App.4th 1271, 1298 (citations 
omitted).) 
As noted above, the Councilmember's business, located on TMA lands, leases hangars, 
rents aircraft, and provides aircraft-maintenance services. There is no indication that the 
development, construction, or operation of the Project would result in any change in demand for the 
aviation services provided by the Councilmember's business. Furthermore, the potential contracts 
for the development, construction, and operation of the Project would not affect the term, 
provisions, or value of the business's long-term leases of the TMA lands underlying the hangars. 
Finally, it does not appear that the potential contracts relating to the Project would affect any source 
ofincome to the Councilmember's business. Therefore, based on the facts presented, we find that 
the Councilmember would not be financially interested in the potential contracts relating to the 
Project for purposes of Section I 090. 5 
5 However, if there is a change in the facts or circumstances relating to these potential contracts, we caution 
that the Councilmember should seek further advice regarding the applicability of Section I 090 in light of those changed 
facts or circumstances. 


Page No .. 8 
Thus, we conclude that Section 1090 does not prohibit the Councilmember from making or 
participating in the making of, or the City from entering into, one or more potential contracts for the 
development, construction, or operation of the Project. Because Section 1090 is not applicable, we 
do not analyze whether a remote or noninterest exception or the rule of necessity applies, and our 
Section-I 090 analysis ends here.

QUESTION: 1. Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Hetge from talcing part in governmental decisions 
related to the development and construction of a motocross track on the City's Event Center & 
Rodeo Grounds within the boundaries of the Tehachapi Municipal Airport (hereafter, the 
"Motocross Track Project")? 
2. Does Section 1090 prohibit the Councilmember from malcing or participating in the 
making of, or the City from entering into, a potential contract or contracts relating to the 
development, construction, or operation of the Motocross Track Project? 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in

CONCLUSION: 1. No. The Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in decisions related to 
the development and construction of the Project because those decisions would not have a 
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the Councilmember's financial interests. 
2. No. Section I 090 does not prohibit the Councilmember from making or participating in 
the making of, or the City from entering into, a potential contract or contracts relating to the 
development, construction, or operation of the Project.

Hetge Tehachapi City Councilman Elect 333 S. Dennison Road, #65 Tehachapi, CA 93561 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-16-264 Dear Mr. Hetge: This letter responds to Tehachapi City Councilmember Kenneth R. Hetge's request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act") and Section 1090.¹ Please note that we do not advise on any other area of law, including Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of interest. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Kern County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).) QUESTIONS 1. Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Hetge from taking part in governmental decisions related to the development and construction of a motocross track on the City's Event Center & Rodeo Grounds within the boundaries of the Tehachapi Municipal Airport (hereafter, the "Motocross Track Project")? 2. Does Section 1090 prohibit the Councilmember from making or participating in the making of, or the City from entering into, a potential contract or contracts relating to the development, construction, or operation of the Motocross Track Project? ___________________________ ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-16-264 Page No. 2 CONCLUSIONS 1. No. The Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in decisions related to the development and construction of the Project because those decisions would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the Councilmember's financial interests. 2. No. Section 1090 does not prohibit the Councilmember from making or participating in the making of, or the City from entering into, a potential contract or contracts relating to the development, construction, or operation of the Project. FACTS Tehachapi City Councilmember Kenneth Hetge was elected to office on November 8, 2016, and his four-year term of office commenced on December 19, 2016. The City owns the Tehachapi Municipal Airport ("TMA"), the TMA is located within the City limits, and
